quote: That was quite a while ago. We haven't haven't talked about it for about six months.
stewart_read: Roger's stale-lead disclosure is a textbook flip opportunity. The six-month gap is not a disqualifier — it is an opening. A rep executing the objection_inversion move would pivot immediately: 'Has your bill changed in the last six months?' or 'A lot of people who looked into it six months ago are coming back now because their summer bills hit.' Holland instead responds with a generic free-estimate pitch, treating the gap as neutral context rather than as leverage. Reactive read holds: no evidence Holland was setting up a later move — the pitch that follows is generic, not gap-specific.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland acknowledged the gap and moved to re-engage with the free estimate offer, which did produce Roger's agreement to proceed — arguably a functional recovery even if not the sharpest available move.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the codex flip mechanic (coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion). The stale-lead gap is one of the most common entry points on recycled leads. The manager should drill: 'What do you say when someone tells you it's been six months?' The answer is a bill-change probe, not a generic pitch.

quote: they looked at things, but, I think they took some measurements or pictures or something, towards the east, but they never got back to us.
stewart_read: Roger is disclosing that a prior solar company came to his home, took measurements, and then ghosted him. This is a trust-damage signal — he was left hanging by a competitor. It is also a verify.prior_designs hit AND a micro-trade opportunity: Holland could have acknowledged the bad experience explicitly ('That's frustrating — you gave them your time and they disappeared') and then used it as a legitimacy bridge ('That's exactly why we do things differently — our specialist follows up with a full design'). Instead, Holland responds with 'Oh, okay' and pivots immediately to the free estimate pitch. The empathy moment is skipped entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's quick pivot to the free estimate could be read as not dwelling on a competitor's failure — keeping the call forward-moving. But the absence of any acknowledgment of Roger's experience means a trust-building moment was left on the table.
coaching_implication: Use this clip to show Holland what a trust-gift looks like. Roger handed over a vulnerability (ghosted by a prior company) and Holland walked past it. The manager should ask: 'What would you say to someone who told you a company came to their house and never called back?' The correct move is acknowledge + bridge to Ion's process, not skip to pitch.

quote: What are your company Well, there's no no charge for that. Right? Not just Yeah. No. It you're not committed to anything.
stewart_read: Roger begins to ask a question about the company — the transcript captures 'What are your company' before the line breaks. Holland cuts across it with the no-charge / no-commitment reassurance before Roger can finish. This is a missed Verify entry point: Roger was about to ask something that would have surfaced either a legitimacy concern or a competitive question. Holland's interruption forecloses the answer before the question is complete. The no-commitment reassurance is not wrong, but it is premature — Holland doesn't know what Roger was actually asking.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have correctly read Roger's incomplete question as a cost/commitment concern and addressed it efficiently. The reassurance did produce Roger's agreement to proceed ('we could take a look at that').
coaching_implication: Drill active listening discipline. The manager should replay this clip and ask: 'What was Roger about to ask?' Holland doesn't know — and neither do we — because Holland talked over it. The habit of pre-empting customer questions with reassurances can mask real concerns that surface later as no-shows or cancellations.

quote: yeah, I see that you have a couple of pretty large trees next to you. Are those trees still there?
stewart_read: Holland's satellite-based tree check is the pivot point of the entire call. The question itself is not wrong — shading is a legitimate site factor. The failure is in what Holland does with the answer. Roger confirms the trees are present, and Holland immediately concludes DQ without asking: (a) whether Roger has plans to remove them, (b) what Roger's electric bill looks like, (c) whether the roof faces other directions, or (d) any other Verify item. The DQ is reached from a single satellite observation and a one-word customer confirmation. This is scope creep into site-assessment territory that belongs to the closer, combined with a premature DQ that bypasses the full qualification process. Strategic read: there is a plausible deliberate-filter reading — Holland may be running a satellite-first triage to avoid wasting closer time on unworkable sites. But even under that reading, the correct triage question is 'Do you have plans to remove those trees?' not a unilateral DQ declaration.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may be executing a legitimate efficiency filter — if satellite data shows heavy shading, a quick confirmation before running the full Verify sequence saves time for both parties. This is a defensible workflow if Ion's process supports it.
coaching_implication: The manager needs to resolve the workflow question first: does Ion's process authorize setters to DQ on satellite shading without completing Verify? If yes, Holland's execution is correct but the tree-removal question is still missing. If no, this is a premature DQ and a lost lead. Either way, the single question Holland should always ask before any shade-based DQ is: 'Do you have any plans to remove those trees?' That question costs 5 seconds and could preserve the lead.

quote: I do think that you have too much shade for solar to be beneficial.
stewart_read: Holland delivers a site-assessment conclusion — 'too much shade for solar to be beneficial' — that is closer-tier content. The setter's job is to qualify and schedule; the determination of whether a specific home's shading profile makes solar unworkable is a technical judgment that belongs to the specialist. Holland is not wrong that shade is a problem, but the unilateral declaration of 'too much shade' without any Verify completion, without asking about tree removal, and without routing to a specialist is a scope violation that ends the call prematurely. Reactive read holds: no evidence this was a deliberate strategic filter — the passive callback offer that follows ('just feel free to reach out') is not the behavior of a rep who has a plan for this lead.
alternative_hypothesis: If Ion's workflow explicitly authorizes setters to DQ on satellite-confirmed heavy shading without running Verify, this is procedurally correct. The coaching gap would then be narrower: the missing tree-removal question only.
coaching_implication: The manager should clarify with Holland: 'What's your process when you see shade on satellite?' If Holland is freelancing a DQ that Ion's process doesn't authorize, this is a training gap on setter scope. If Holland is following a workflow, the gap is the missing tree-removal probe. Either way, the line 'I do think that you have too much shade for solar to be beneficial' should never be the sentence that ends a call — that judgment belongs to the closer.

quote: If you ever plan on cutting those down or anything, just feel free to reach out, and we'd love to help you out.
stewart_read: Holland's re-engagement offer is maximally passive: no date, no follow-up mechanism, no curiosity hook, no bill anchor, no name capture for a callback. 'Just feel free to reach out' places the entire burden of re-engagement on the customer. Per the codex, the delta between a callback with a locked time and 'we'll touch base' is major on no-show rate — the same logic applies here to re-engagement rate. A rep executing a deliberate filter strategy would at minimum plant a specific trigger: 'If you do decide to take those down, call us within 30 days and we'll prioritize your design.' Holland plants nothing.
alternative_hypothesis: If the lead is genuinely unworkable due to shading, a passive close-out is arguably appropriate — there is no appointment to book and no immediate next step. The coaching gap is then about whether the DQ itself was premature, not about the close-out language.
coaching_implication: Even on a DQ'd lead, the close-out should plant a specific re-engagement trigger. The manager should drill: 'What do you say at the end of a call where the home doesn't qualify today but might qualify later?' The answer should include a specific condition ('when the trees come down'), a specific action ('call us'), and ideally a specific timeframe or incentive. 'Feel free to reach out' is not a re-engagement strategy.

quote: yeah, I see that you have a couple of pretty large trees next to you. Are those trees still there? Yes.
stewart_read: Roger's confirmation that the trees are still present is not itself an enthusiasm signal — but the trees' existence as the sole DQ factor is. If Roger were to remove those trees, the home becomes a viable solar candidate. Holland identifies the trees as a barrier but never probes whether removal is planned or possible. The enthusiasm signal here is the inverse: the barrier is removable, which means the lead has latent value. A rep attuned to enthusiasm signals would hear 'trees are still there' and immediately ask 'Are those something you've thought about removing?' — converting a DQ trigger into a conditional qualification. Holland hears it as confirmation of DQ and exits.
alternative_hypothesis: Roger's one-word 'Yes' gives Holland no signal that tree removal is under consideration. Holland may have reasonably concluded that without any indication of removal plans, the lead is not workable.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to teach the 'removable barrier' concept. Not every DQ trigger is permanent. The manager should ask Holland: 'What's the difference between a barrier that ends the call and a barrier that creates a conditional lead?' Trees, unlike a bad roof with no replacement plans or a non-homeowner status, are removable. The probe question ('Any plans to take those down?') is the difference between a dead lead and a future sit.
